stem: Bipolar I Disorder is diagnosed when an individual experiences at least one manic episode. A manic episode is defined as a distinct period of abnormally elevated, expansive, or irritable mood that lasts for at least how long OR requires hospitalization?
options: At least one week or requires hospitalization
At least four consecutive days or requires hospitalization
At least two weeks or requires hospitalization

stem: Bipolar II Disorder is distinguished from Bipolar I by the nature of its manic episodes. Which statement MOST accurately describes what distinguishes a hypomanic episode from a full manic episode in Bipolar II?
options: A hypomanic episode lasts at least four consecutive days, is associated with noticeable changes in functioning, is less severe than full mania, and does not typically require hospitalization — individuals with Bipolar II have never experienced a full manic episode

A hypomanic episode lasts at least one week, requires hospitalization, and is characterized by inflated self-esteem, decreased need for sleep, and engagement in risky behaviours — the same as Bipolar I but with depressive episodes in between
A hypomanic episode lasts at least one week, does not require hospitalization, and involves symptoms that are identical to a full manic episode but occur only in the context of substance use

stem: Cyclothymic Disorder is described in the text as a milder form of bipolar disorder. Which statement MOST accurately describes what Cyclothymic Disorder involves?
options: Characterized by chronic mood instability with numerous periods of depressive symptoms and hypomanic symptoms over a period of at least two years, without meeting full criteria for a major depressive episode, hypomanic episode, or manic episode
Characterized by at least one full manic episode and multiple major depressive episodes over two years, with intervening periods of normal mood lasting less than two months
Characterized by hypomanic episodes only lasting at least four days, without any depressive symptoms, occurring in a cyclical pattern over at least one year

stem: Mania is described as primary or secondary in nature. Which statement MOST accurately distinguishes Primary from Secondary mania?
options: Primary mania is an affective disorder (mood disorder); secondary mania occurs secondary to a variety of organic disorders such as drug intake, infection, neoplasm, epilepsy, or metabolic disturbances
Primary mania is caused by organic disorders such as drug intake and infection; secondary mania is a pure affective disorder with no identifiable organic aetiology
Primary mania requires hospitalization while secondary mania can be managed in outpatient settings; the distinction is based on severity rather than aetiology

stem: The biochemical cause of mania specifically involves which neurotransmitters and what change in their levels?
options: An increase in the production of biogenic amines — specifically norepinephrine and serotonin in the brain — as a biochemical factor
A decrease in the production of biogenic amines — specifically dopamine and acetylcholine — leading to disinhibition of mood regulation centres
An imbalance in sodium and chloride ions across neuronal membranes leading to excessive firing of neurons in the limbic system

stem: An increase in salt content above normal in the body is listed as a cause of mania. What specific electrolyte is named and what is the consequence?
options: Sodium (Na) may lead to excessive activity of neurons in the brain
Potassium (K) may lead to excessive depolarisation of neuronal cell membranes causing manic excitement
Calcium (Ca) may lead to excessive neurotransmitter release from presynaptic terminals causing disinhibited mood

stem: Hypomania is distinguished from Manic Excitement in the text. Which pair CORRECTLY contrasts the two forms of mania?
options: Hypomania: the patient is euphoric, elated, dressing colourfully, cracking jokes, talking excessively and over active; Manic excitement: the patient is very irritable, excited, violent, and is a nuisance to the public

Hypomania: the patient is very irritable, excited, violent and is a nuisance to the public; Manic excitement: the patient is euphoric, elated, dressing colourfully, cracking jokes, and talking excessively

Hypomania: the patient has inflated self-esteem and decreased need for sleep without disturbing others; Manic excitement: the patient requires hospitalization due to extreme grandiosity and flight of ideas

stem: Flight of Ideas is listed as a clinical manifestation of mania. Which statement MOST accurately describes this symptom as it appears in a manic patient?
options: A subjective experience where thoughts are racing — the patient's associations move rapidly from one idea to another
The patient becomes easily distracted by multiple, unimportant, or irrelevant external stimuli, shifting attention constantly
The patient invents new meaningless words and stringing unrelated ideas together with no apparent logical connection

stem: Regarding drug therapy for Mania, which of the following schedules is CORRECTLY stated in the text for Largactil?
options: Largactil 100mg three times daily; Haloperidol 5m twice a day; IM Haloperidol 5-10mg 2 hourly till patient is sedated or maximum of 5mg and change to tablet Haloperidol 1.5-long three times a day
Largactil 50-100mg stat then tablets 100-200mg three times daily; IM Chlorpromazine 50-100mg to start followed by tablet Chlorpromazine 100-200mg three times a day to be reduced gradually
Largactil 50-100mg stat then tablets 100-200mg three times daily; IM Haloperidol 5-10mg 2 hourly till patient is sedated or maximum of 5mg and change to tablet Haloperidol 1.5-long three times a day

stem: Lithium carbonate (mood stabilizer) is specifically mentioned in the text for which patient population?
options: Used for patients who suffer frequent relapses of mania
Used for all first-episode manic patients as first-line mood stabilising treatment
Used exclusively for patients who have failed two trials of antipsychotic medication

stem: In the management of a manic patient, the nurse is instructed regarding the ward environment. Which statement MOST accurately reflects the text's instruction?
options: The ward must be quiet and pleasant; factors that irritate the patient like excessive noise, bright colours etc are to be avoided; separate rooms may be ideal with simple furnishing; fluctuation of mood states must be watched; active games, ward occupation and creative work will channelise his energy

The ward should have stimulating activities including music and social interaction to distract the patient from grandiose ideation; the nurse should encourage group activities and peer interaction to reduce social isolation
The ward should be brightly lit with multiple stimulating activities to reduce the patient's agitation; the nurse should engage the patient in competitive games to redirect excess energy

stem: Depression is defined in the text as an affective disorder. Which of the following MOST completely and accurately defines depression as stated?
options: An affective disorder characterized by extreme feeling of sadness, withdrawal, excessive psychomotor retardation and suicidal thought; OR an affective disorder with retardation of mental, physical and social activities characterized by loss of interest, lack of energy and pessimism
An affective disorder characterized by persistent low mood, anhedonia, and cognitive impairment lasting at least two weeks, without any psychomotor changes or suicidal ideation
An affective disorder characterized by extreme feeling of sadness and suicidal thought only, caused by biochemical imbalance in monoamine neurotransmitters in the prefrontal cortex

stem: Endogenous Depression (Major Depression) is defined by the presence of a depressed mood or pleasure plus 4 or more of seven symptoms. Which of the following is NOT one of the seven symptoms listed for Endogenous Depression?
options: Disorganized speech and thought insertion — symptoms of psychotic features that may accompany severe major depressive episodes

Loss of interest and pleasure; loss of worthlessness of guilt; loss of energy and fatigue; impaired concentration; thought of suicide; loss or increase of appetite and weight; insomnia or excessive sleep and retardation or agitation
Thought of suicide; loss or increase of appetite; retardation or agitation

stem: Reactive/Neurotic/Dysthymic disorder requires depressed mood for most of the day for at least how long, plus presence of at least how many of six symptoms?
options: Depressed mood for most of the day for at least two years; presence of at least two of the following six symptoms
Depressed mood for most of the day for at least one year; presence of at least three of the following six symptoms
Depressed mood for most of the day for at least six months; presence of at least four of the following six symptoms

stem: Involutional/Melancholia Depression occurs at which stage of life and is caused by which hormonal event? Which statement is MOST accurate?
options: Occurs in the middle age and later third of life; caused by hormonal changes in the body during the menopausal stage in predisposed women; characterized by agitation, complaints of body or mental suffering, restlessness, hypochondriacal ideas and delusion of guilt and unworthiness
Occurs in early adulthood between 18-35 years of age; caused by biochemical imbalances in monoamine neurotransmitters; characterized by psychomotor retardation, suicidal ideation, and hypersomnia
Occurs in adolescence between 12-18 years of age; caused by pubertal hormonal changes affecting the limbic system; characterized by irritability, school refusal, and somatic complaints

stem: Senile Depression occurs from 70 years and above. Which specific features distinguish it from other types of depression according to the text?
options: The risk of suicides is much increased and it may be irreversible; signs/symptoms include degeneration of intellectual power, concentration and memory; there is also loss of appetite, loss of weight and hallucinations
The risk of suicide is lower than in other forms of depression because patients lack the energy to act on suicidal ideation; it may be reversible with antidepressant therapy within 4-6 weeks
The risk of suicide is equivalent to that of endogenous depression; it is always reversible with ECT; characterised primarily by delusions of persecution and auditory hallucinations

stem: Major Depressive Disorder (MDD) as classified by DSM-5 requires symptoms to be present for at least how long?
options: Symptoms must be present for at least two weeks
Symptoms must be present for at least one month
Symptoms must be present for at least two years

stem: Persistent Depressive Disorder (PDD), formerly known as dysthymia, is described in the text. Which statement MOST accurately characterises PDD compared to MDD?
options: PDD is a chronic form of depression that lasts for at least two years; individuals with PDD may experience symptoms similar to MDD but at a milder intensity; symptoms may persist for long periods with brief periods of improvement
PDD is a more severe form of depression than MDD requiring hospitalization; it lasts for at least six months and is characterized by psychotic features such as hallucinations and delusions
PDD is characterised by depressive episodes alternating with hypomanic episodes over at least two years, meeting criteria for Cyclothymic Disorder on the bipolar spectrum

stem: Disruptive Mood Dysregulation Disorder (DMDD) is described in the text. Which population is it specifically diagnosed in and what are its characteristic features?
options: Typically diagnosed in children and adolescents; characterized by severe and recurrent temper outbursts inconsistent with the child's developmental level; accompanied by persistent irritability between the outbursts
Typically diagnosed in adults between 18-35 years; characterized by rapid mood cycling between depression and hypomania within the same day; accompanied by impulsive behaviour and self-harm
Typically diagnosed in elderly patients above 65 years; characterized by recurrent episodes of tearfulness and emotional lability; accompanied by cognitive impairment and functional decline

stem: Premenstrual Dysphoric Disorder (PMDD) is described in the text. Which statement MOST accurately characterises when it occurs and what it involves?
options: A type of depression that occurs in women during the premenstrual phase of their menstrual cycle; involves symptoms such as mood swings, irritability, depression, anxiety, and physical discomfort that significantly disrupt daily functioning
A type of depression that occurs in women immediately after delivery; characterized by severe mood swings, crying, and inability to bond with the baby, lasting up to one year postpartum
A type of depression that occurs in women during the menopausal transition; characterized by hypochondriacal ideas, delusions of guilt, and excessive complaints of body or mental suffering

stem: ECT is listed in the text as used in the management of depression. For which specific depressive presentation is ECT described as preferable?
options: ECT is preferable for depression with suicidal tendency
ECT is preferable for depression that has not responded to two trials of antidepressant medication
ECT is preferable for depression associated with psychotic features such as hallucinations and delusions

stem: The nursing care for a depressed patient includes specific precautions to prevent suicide. Which of the following is listed in the text as a precaution to prevent suicide?
options: Open suicidal caution card; remove harmful objects from patient vicinity; ensure patient swallows drugs instantly so as not to board them; naked electric wires in the ward must be repaired; medical cupboard must be locked always; monitor patient when taking bath; observe patient closely

Place the patient in an open ward without access to sharp objects; monitor the patient every 15 minutes; administer antidepressants at bedtime to reduce daytime sedation; encourage family visits daily
Restrict the patient to the ward; assign a one-to-one nursing companion at all times; confiscate all personal belongings on admission; place the patient in a seclusion room for safety

stem: Anorexia Nervosa is defined in the text. Which definition MOST completely and accurately describes it as stated in both ICD-11 and DSM-5?
options: A persistent restriction of energy intake leading to a significantly low body weight in the context of age, sex, developmental trajectory, and physical health; characterized by an intense fear of gaining weight or becoming fat or persistent behaviour that interferes with weight gain, even at a significantly low weight

A disorder characterized by recurrent episodes of binge eating in which an individual eats an unusually large amount of food in a discrete period of time and experiences a sense of lack of control over eating during the episode

A clinical syndrome characterized by extreme overeating followed by self-induced vomiting and abuse of laxatives and diuretics; occurs predominantly in females and begins in adolescence or early adult life

stem: The Restricting Type of Anorexia Nervosa is distinguished from the Binge-Eating/Purging Type. Which statement MOST accurately describes the Restricting Type?
options: During the current episode, weight loss is achieved by restricting calories following drastic diets, fasting, and excessive exercise; the person has not regularly engaged in binge-eating or purging behaviour such as self-induced vomiting or misuse of laxatives, diuretics, or enemas

During the current episode, the patient engages in binge eating, consuming large amounts of food in a short period of time, and attempts to compensate for calories consumed by purging through vomiting, using laxatives or diuretics, or excessive exercise

During the current episode, the person uses inappropriate compensatory behaviours other than purging to control their weight such as fasting or excessive exercise — the non-purging compensatory strategy

stem: Perfectionism is listed as a cause of Anorexia Nervosa. How does it contribute to the development of eating disorders according to the text?
options: People with perfectionistic tendencies may be more prone to developing eating disorders as they strive for an unattainable ideal of perfection, including their body shape and weight
People with perfectionistic tendencies may develop eating disorders because they set unrealistic academic and professional goals, leaving no time for adequate nutrition
People with perfectionistic tendencies are more prone to developing Bulimia Nervosa specifically, because perfectionism drives the binge-purge cycle as a compensation for dietary failures

stem: Signs and Symptoms of Anorexia Nervosa include which finding regarding the patient's preoccupation with food despite refusing to eat?
options: Preoccupation with food: the patient thinks and talks about food at great length, prepares enormous amounts of food for friends and family members but refuses to eat
Preoccupation with food: the patient binges on large amounts of food in secret and then purges immediately afterward through self-induced vomiting
Preoccupation with food: the patient counts calories obsessively and reads nutrition labels on all food products but maintains a slightly underweight BMI

stem: Which of the following physical consequences of Anorexia Nervosa is CORRECTLY identified in the text?
options: Amenorrhea in females who have reached puberty; dry skin, brittle nails, and hair loss; low blood pressure, slow heart rate, and electrolyte imbalances; hypothermia, bradycardia, hypotension, and anaemia
Erosion of tooth enamel due to gastric acid in the vomitus; dehydration and electrolyte imbalance due to excessive vomiting and laxative abuse; body image disturbance
Marked weight loss; enlargement of parotid glands; calluses on knuckles (Russell's sign) from self-induced vomiting; metabolic alkalosis from purging

stem: Bulimia Nervosa is described in the text as an eating disorder. Which definition MOST accurately describes it as per the DSM-5?
options: A disorder characterized by recurrent episodes of binge eating in which an individual eats an unusually large amount of food in a discrete period of time and experiences a sense of lack of control over eating during the episode
A disorder characterized by a persistent restriction of energy intake leading to a significantly low body weight, with an intense fear of gaining weight and gross distortion of body image
A disorder characterized by extreme overeating without any compensatory purging behaviour, with the patient experiencing persistent preoccupation with body image and weight

stem: The Purging Type of Bulimia Nervosa is distinguished from the Non-Purging Type. Which pair CORRECTLY contrasts these two types?
options: Purging type: engaged in self-induced vomiting or misuse of laxatives, diuretics, or enemas; Non-purging type: during the current episode, the person uses inappropriate compensatory behaviours other than purging to control their weight such as fasting or excessive exercise
Purging type: uses fasting and excessive exercise to compensate for binge eating; Non-purging type: uses self-induced vomiting or laxatives to compensate for binge eating episodes
Purging type: restricts caloric intake between episodes of binge eating; Non-purging type: engages in binge eating without any compensatory behaviour whatsoever

stem: The Social-Cultural factor listed as a cause of Bulimia Nervosa is described in the text as which of the following?
options: Exposure to values and beliefs about thinness — 'the thinner you look, the more beautiful you look' — and marital conflict
Peer pressure to conform to certain body standards promoted by media and fashion industries, leading to extreme calorie restriction
Cultural practices related to food restriction during religious observances, creating cycles of fasting and compensatory binge eating

stem: The Psychoanalytic Factor listed as a cause of Bulimia Nervosa is described in the text as:
options: Fixation in an earlier development level
Retarded ego development leading to immature coping mechanisms
Unresolved dependence needs creating dependency on food as an emotional regulator

stem: Which sign or symptom of Bulimia Nervosa MOST specifically relates to what happens after a binge episode begins, as stated in the text?
options: After the binge has begun, there is often a feeling of loss of control or inability to stop eating; eating binges may be viewed as pleasurable but are followed by intense self-criticism and depressed mood
After the binge has begun, the patient immediately induces vomiting to prevent caloric absorption; the purging brings temporary relief followed by shame and renewed restriction
After the binge has begun, the patient experiences euphoria followed by anxiety; the anxiety drives the compensatory exercise that characterises the non-purging type

stem: The physical complication unique to Bulimia Nervosa (not Anorexia) related to repeated vomiting is which of the following as stated in the text?
options: Erosion of tooth enamel due to gastric acid in the vomitus; dehydration and electrolyte imbalance due to excessive vomiting and laxative or diuretic abuse
Amenorrhea due to extreme calorie restriction reducing oestrogen production; dry skin, brittle nails, and hair loss from nutritional deficiencies
Marked weight loss leading to significantly low body weight; bradycardia and hypotension from cardiac muscle wasting

stem: The nursing management of eating disorders involves a multidisciplinary approach. The primary goal of treatment is to help individuals achieve which outcome?
options: Recover from the eating disorder and achieve and maintain a healthy weight and eating patterns
Achieve a BMI within the normal range as the sole indicator of treatment success, regardless of eating behaviour
Achieve complete remission of all psychiatric symptoms including depression and anxiety before addressing the eating behaviour

stem: Medical Stabilization is listed as a management strategy for eating disorders. For which patient group is it specifically indicated, and what may it involve?
options: For individuals with severe eating disorders, medical stabilization may be necessary to address immediate health risks; may involve hospitalization or intensive medical monitoring to manage complications such as electrolyte imbalances, heart abnormalities, or organ damage
For all individuals with eating disorders regardless of severity, medical stabilization involves weekly outpatient monitoring of weight, electrolytes, and vital signs
For individuals with Bulimia Nervosa only, medical stabilization involves administration of antiemetics and proton pump inhibitors to prevent further erosion of tooth enamel

stem: CASE SCENARIO: A 17-year-old girl is brought to the psychiatric clinic by her mother. She weighs 35kg (BMI 14), refuses to eat despite preparing elaborate meals for the family, says she 'feels fat,' and has not menstruated in 8 months. She exercises for 3 hours daily. Which diagnosis is MOST consistent?
options: Anorexia Nervosa — Restricting Type: characterized by persistent restriction of energy intake, intense fear of gaining weight, gross distortion of body image, amenorrhea, and excessive exercise without binge-purge behaviour
Bulimia Nervosa — Non-purging Type: characterized by binge eating with compensatory excessive exercise, body image disturbance, and menstrual irregularities
Anorexia Nervosa — Binge-Eating/Purging Type: characterized by recurrent episodes of binge eating and purging through excessive exercise, despite being significantly underweight

stem: CASE SCENARIO (continued from Q36): The patient is admitted. The nurse plans her care. Which nursing action is MOST immediately critical based on the text's management principles?
options: Assess the patient; establish nurse/patient relationship; monitor and record eating behaviours; consider medical stabilization for electrolyte imbalances, heart abnormalities, or organ damage given her severe low weight
Administer antidepressants immediately as depression is the underlying cause of food restriction; encourage the patient to join a group therapy session on the first day of admission
Restrict the patient's access to the exercise room; weigh her daily in the presence of her mother; discourage her from preparing meals for the family as this reinforces her food preoccupation

stem: CASE SCENARIO: A 35-year-old woman presents with a three-week history of profound sadness, early morning waking, weight loss, inability to concentrate, and thoughts of suicide. She states 'life is not worth living.' Which type of depression is MOST consistent with her presentation?
options: Endogenous Depression (Major Depressive Disorder) — characterized by persistent feeling of sadness, hopelessness, and loss of interest or pleasure; symptoms including insomnia (early morning waking), weight loss, impaired concentration, and suicidal tendency are present
Reactive/Neurotic/Dysthymic Disorder — characterized by depressed mood for most of the day for at least two years with at least two of six symptoms; less severe than endogenous depression
Involutional/Melancholia — occurring in middle age during the menopausal stage in predisposed women; characterized by agitation, hypochondriacal ideas, and delusions of guilt

stem: CASE SCENARIO: A 28-year-old man is admitted following an episode of extreme irritability, violence, and being a nuisance in the community. He has been found spending all his money, sleeping only 2 hours a night, and is dressing in bright, mismatched clothing. He is described by his family as 'acting like a different person.' Which form of mania does this presentation MOST represent?
options: Manic Excitement — the patient is very irritable, excited, violent, and is a nuisance to the public
Hypomania — the patient is euphoric, elated, dressing colourfully, cracking jokes, talking excessively and over active
Bipolar II Disorder — the patient is experiencing a hypomanic episode that does not require hospitalization

stem: CASE SCENARIO: A 22-year-old woman with Bulimia Nervosa is admitted. The nurse notes erosion of her tooth enamel and calluses on her knuckles. She admits to eating very large amounts of food rapidly and then vomiting. She feels ashamed after each episode. Which type of Bulimia Nervosa does this represent?
options: Purging Type — the patient is engaged in self-induced vomiting or misuse of laxatives, diuretics, or enemas as a compensatory behaviour
Non-purging Type — the patient uses compensatory behaviours other than purging such as fasting or excessive exercise to control weight
Restricting Type — the patient restricts caloric intake between episodes of binge eating without engaging in any purging behaviour

stem: CASE SCENARIO: A nurse is reviewing a manic patient's drug therapy. The patient has been started on IM Chlorpromazine 50-100mg. According to the text, what should this be followed by?
options: Tablet Chlorpromazine 100-200mg three times a day, to be reduced gradually
Tablet Haloperidol 1.5-long three times a day as the standard step-down from IM Chlorpromazine
Tablet Largactil 50-100mg stat, then tablets 100-200mg three times daily

stem: CASE SCENARIO: A 45-year-old woman with no prior psychiatric history presents with a 3-year history of depressed mood for most of the day, decreased sleep, low energy, low self-esteem, and poor concentration. She denies suicidal ideation and has never had a major depressive episode. Which DSM-5 diagnosis is MOST consistent?
options: Persistent Depressive Disorder (PDD) — formerly dysthymia; a chronic form of depression lasting at least two years with milder symptoms than MDD and brief periods of improvement
Major Depressive Disorder (MDD) — characterized by persistent sadness, hopelessness, and loss of interest with symptoms present for at least two weeks
Cyclothymic Disorder — characterized by chronic mood instability with numerous periods of depressive and hypomanic symptoms over at least two years

stem: CASE SCENARIO: A nurse is monitoring a depressed patient at meal time. The patient says he is not hungry and has not eaten in two days. Which nursing action is MOST consistent with the management of depression described in the text?
options: Encourage stimulating activities around the patient; give nourishing diet especially fluid diet; give drug therapy as prescribed; encourage the patient to take care of personal hygiene
Force feed the patient using nasogastric tube to prevent further weight loss; document the caloric intake and report any refusal to the physician immediately
Allow the patient to skip meals if he is not hungry as forced feeding may worsen his depression; focus instead on addressing suicidal ideation as the priority

stem: Bipolar I Disorder is diagnosed when an individual experiences at least one manic episode lasting at least ____________ week(s) or requires ____________.

Bipolar II Disorder requires both major depressive episodes and hypomanic episodes. A hypomanic episode lasts at least ____________ consecutive days and does not typically require ____________. Individuals with Bipolar II have never experienced a full ____________ episode.

Cyclothymic Disorder is characterized by chronic mood instability with depressive and hypomanic symptoms over a period of at least ____________ years, without meeting full criteria for a ____________ episode, hypomanic episode, or manic episode.

Primary mania is an ____________ disorder (mood disorder). Secondary mania occurs secondary to a variety of ____________ disorders such as drug intake, infection, neoplasm, epilepsy, or metabolic disturbances.
Hypomania is a form of mania where the patient is ____________, elated, dressing ____________, cracking jokes, and talking excessively and over-active.
Lithium ____________ (mood stabilizer) is used for patients who suffer ____________ relapses of mania.
Depression is defined as an affective disorder characterized by extreme feeling of ____________, withdrawal, excessive psychomotor ____________ and suicidal thought.

Endogenous Depression requires a depressed mood or pleasure plus ____________ or more of seven symptoms including loss of interest and pleasure, loss of worthlessness of guilt, loss of energy and fatigue, impaired concentration, thought of suicide, loss or increase of appetite, and ____________ or excessive sleep.
Reactive/Neurotic/Dysthymic disorder requires depressed mood for most of the day for at least ____________ years, plus the presence of at least ____________ of six symptoms.

Involutional/Melancholia occurs in the middle age and later third of life; it is caused by ____________ changes during the ____________ stage in predisposed women. It is characterized by agitation, hypochondriacal ideas, and delusion of ____________ and unworthiness.
Senile Depression occurs from ____________ years and above. The risk of suicides is ____________ increased and it may be ____________.
ECT is preferable for depression with ____________ tendency. Drug therapy for depression includes anti-depressant drugs such as trypitizol ____________-____________ per day.

Anorexia Nervosa is defined as a persistent ____________ of energy intake leading to a significantly low body weight; characterized by an intense ____________ of gaining weight or becoming fat or persistent behaviour that interferes with ____________ gain.

In Anorexia Nervosa — Restricting Type, weight loss is achieved by restricting calories following drastic diets, ____________, and excessive exercise; the person has NOT regularly engaged in ____________-eating or ____________ behaviour.

Bulimia Nervosa is described as the ____________-and-____________ syndrome, characterized by extreme overeating followed by self-induced vomiting and abuse of laxatives and diuretics. It occurs predominantly in ____________ and begins in adolescence or early adult life.

In Bulimia Nervosa — Non-purging Type, during the current episode the person uses inappropriate ____________ behaviours OTHER than purging to control their weight, such as ____________ or excessive exercise.
The psychoanalytic factor for Bulimia Nervosa is ____________ in an earlier development level, while the behavioural factor is unresolved ____________ needs.
A physical complication specific to repeated purging in Bulimia Nervosa is erosion of ____________ enamel due to ____________ acid in the vomitus.

In the nursing management of a manic patient, two manic patients should ____________ be nursed side by side. The nurse should use a ____________ sound tone and ____________ should be more effective than arguments.

In preventing suicide in a depressed patient, the nurse must open a ____________ caution card, remove ____________ objects from the patient's vicinity, ensure the patient ____________ drugs instantly so as not to board them.

CASE SCENARIO (Fill-in): A 16-year-old girl refuses to eat despite preparing large meals for others, says she 'feels fat,' weighs only 34kg, and has not menstruated for 7 months. This is consistent with ____________ Nervosa — ____________ Type, characterised by persistent restriction of energy intake and an intense fear of gaining ____________.

CASE SCENARIO (Fill-in): A 25-year-old woman with Bulimia Nervosa feels a loss of ____________ during binge episodes and intense ____________ and depressed mood afterward. She is engaged in ____________-induced vomiting, classifying her as the Purging Type.

CASE SCENARIO (Fill-in): A 38-year-old man with Bipolar I is admitted during a manic episode. He has not slept for 5 days and is violent. The nurse prepares drug therapy including IM ____________ 5-10mg 2 hourly until patient is sedated or maximum of 5mg, then changes to tablet ____________ 1.5-long three times a day.
CASE SCENARIO: A nurse is teaching a student about ECT use in a manic patient. The student asks when ECT is used for mania. The nurse correctly states:
options: ECT is also used in manic excitement when patients are very irritable, excited, and violent and it is a nuisance to the public
ECT is used only for treatment-resistant depression and is never indicated for mania under any circumstances
ECT is used for mania only when Lithium carbonate has failed after three adequate trials at therapeutic blood levels

stem: CASE SCENARIO: A 14-year-old boy has severe temper outbursts three times a week that are inconsistent with his developmental level. Between outbursts, he is persistently irritable. His parents report this has been ongoing for over one year. Which DSM-5 diagnosis does the nurse anticipate?
options: Disruptive Mood Dysregulation Disorder (DMDD) — typically diagnosed in children and adolescents; characterized by severe and recurrent temper outbursts inconsistent with developmental level; accompanied by persistent irritability between outbursts
Bipolar I Disorder — characterized by at least one manic episode lasting at least one week; the irritability and explosive outbursts represent manic episodes in a paediatric presentation
Cyclothymic Disorder — characterized by chronic mood instability with hypomanic and depressive symptoms over at least two years; the outbursts represent hypomanic episodes

stem: CASE SCENARIO: A nurse notes that a patient with depression has been hiding her medications under her tongue instead of swallowing them. Which specific nursing precaution does the text identify to address this?
options: Ensure that the patient swallows her drugs instantly so as not to board them — this is specifically listed as a precaution to prevent suicide in depressed patients
Crush all medications and dissolve them in a small amount of water before administration to prevent the patient from hiding tablets under the tongue
Administer all medications via intramuscular route for depressed patients to ensure compliance and prevent accumulation of tablets for a potential overdose

stem: CASE SCENARIO: A patient with Anorexia Nervosa tells the nurse she needs to exercise for 2 hours after eating 'even a small snack' because she feels guilty about the calories. The nurse recognises this as consistent with which type of Anorexia Nervosa and which specific feature?
options: Anorexia Nervosa — Binge-Eating/Purging Type: the patient engages in excessive exercise as a compensatory purging mechanism after consuming food, consistent with compensatory behaviour following eating
Anorexia Nervosa — Restricting Type: weight loss achieved by restricting calories, fasting, and excessive exercise; the person has not regularly engaged in binge-eating or purging behaviour
Bulimia Nervosa — Non-purging Type: the patient uses excessive exercise as an inappropriate compensatory behaviour other than purging to control weight
